rewritten_text: Google Ads optimization: with a proven ROAS (13:1 at $5k/month spend), scale spend infinitely by optimizing the funnel first, then increasing budget. Example: after landing-page + ad funnel optimization, estimated ROAS increases to 26:1, enabling scale from $5k to $30k/month.
notes: A5/A6: Framework and Claims sections atomized into bullets; merge back to prose.
Edge text is clear: improves close rate first, then raises prices. Named comparison, resolvable actors.

rewritten_text: Pest control company at $50M ARR targeting $1B (20x growth) identifies delivery-team retention as the constraint (not recruitment). Solution: expand career ladder from 3 rungs to 9–18 advancement steps, spaced 3–6 months apart, to reduce churn and increase avg. tenure.
notes: A3: bare numbers '9-18 rungs', '3-6 months' without axis—what's the baseline industry ladder? What retention % uplift is expected?

rewritten_text: Alternative mortgage model (Easy Dwell): investor buys residential property with 2% seller financing or subject-to mortgage, transfers title to buyer's LLC, collects $25K upfront + $800/month for 30–40 years (~60% cash-on-cash return). Compare: traditional mortgage buyer pays 5-6.5% interest; this model yields 12-15% IRR to investor.
notes: A1: 'buyer' and 'owner' are unresolved roles; A3: '$25K + $800/month' and '60%' lack comparison axis.

rewritten_text: Gratification timing / Third Marshmallow Fallacy: delaying gratification indefinitely wastes capital and human lifespan. Framework by career stage: early stage (0–5 years in business), longer delay correlates with bigger outcomes (~10x return per year delayed); later stage (5+ years), risk-adjust future cash flows using discount rate 10-15%. Example: $100M today > $uncertain $1B 10 years away (5-year runway to deploy capital). Personal anecdote: $4K/month cash from PT client unlocked survival during gym-launch phase (non-scalable but immediate).
notes: A3: '$100M today' vs. '$1B 10 years' lacks discount rate or survival/runway context; A4: 'must be in the middle' is vague hortative.

rewritten_text: BCBA test-prep business with 75% gross margins and $80K/month revenue ($960K/year ARR) targets $1M/year; constraints are (1) proof of graduate employment outcomes to universities, (2) exclusive university partnerships to lock in volume. New revenue model: career placement service capturing 15-20% of grad first-year salary.
notes: A3: '$80K/month', '75% margins', '$1M/year' are bare numbers; what's the path from $960K to $1M? What's the placement take-rate baseline?
Clear mechanism: join community → say words → get approval → keep saying them (belief follows reward). Load-bearing. Row 1,2,3 pass; 5,7 N/A for topic notes.

rewritten_text: Health insurance broker industry analysis: commission-based model misaligns incentives—brokers profit from volume/complexity, not client outcomes; Buffett calls this a scam. Better model: centers-of-influence partnerships (e.g., CPAs, financial advisors) who refer clients directly, shifting margin from commissions to relationship value.
notes: A18: 'broken value proposition' vs. 'better value proposition' lacks metric (which dimension? cost? transparency? client satisfaction?).
